title: Turrón de Alicante
subtitle: Spanish hard nougat with whole almonds and honey
excerpt: Turrón de Alicante is the hard nougat of southeastern Spain: a brittle slab of cooked honey and sugar set with whole blanched almonds and bound with egg white. When you bite into it, it shatters into shards and the almonds are dry and crisp throughout. It is made at Christmas, sold in rectangular slabs, and cut with a heavy knife at the table. Patience is required at the cooking stage; the honey must reach hard crack stage before the almonds go in.
sourceNotes: Turrón is one of the oldest confections of the Iberian Peninsula, with documented production in Xixona and Alicante dating to the early sixteenth century. It is mentioned in the municipal records of Alicante from 1582. Two types are protected by geographical indication: turrón de Xixona (soft, paste-like) and turrón de Alicante (hard, with whole almonds). The almond and honey combination is traced to Arab confectionery introduced to the Peninsula during the Moorish period.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 slab (about 20 × 10 cm), cuts into 15 to 20 pieces
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Turrón keeps for 6 weeks in an airtight container at room temperature. Humidity is the enemy — store away from moisture.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required; cooked on the hob to hard crack stage

glossaryTerms.definition: A stage in sugar cookery reached at around 149 to 154 degrees C (300 to 310 degrees F), where the sugar solution contains almost no water. Dropped into cold water, it forms rigid, brittle threads that snap cleanly. Hard crack is required for turrón, pulled sugar, boiled sweets, and toffee apples. At lower temperatures the candy will be chewy; above 154 degrees C it begins to caramelise. A sugar thermometer is the reliable method — thread and water tests require practice.

content.text: The cooked honey and sugar mixture must reach

before the nuts and egg whites go in; if it has not reached temperature, the turrón will be chewy rather than brittle. Work quickly once the almonds and egg whites are folded in: the mixture seizes fast. Pour immediately into the lined mould and level with a wet spatula.

Line a 20 × 10 cm tin or mould with parchment and lightly oil the paper. Toast 300 g blanched almonds in a dry pan over medium heat until lightly golden, about 6 minutes. Set aside. Combine 250 g honey, 150 g caster sugar, and 50 g glucose syrup in a heavy-based saucepan over medium heat. Stir to dissolve, then stop stirring and cook to 149 to 154 degrees C on a sugar thermometer; hard crack stage. While the syrup cooks, whisk 2 egg whites to stiff peaks in a heatproof bowl. Working quickly and carefully, pour the hot syrup in a thin stream over the egg whites, whisking constantly. The mixture will seize and become very stiff immediately. Fold in the warm toasted almonds with a wooden spoon. Tip immediately into the prepared tin and level with a damp spatula, pressing firmly. Leave to set at room temperature or refrigerate for 4 hours until completely hard. Cut into pieces with a heavy knife.

items.problem: Turrón is chewy rather than brittle
items.solution: The syrup did not reach hard crack stage. Test with a thermometer — 149 degrees C minimum. A drop in ice water should form a rigid thread that snaps. If it bends, keep cooking.

items.problem: Sugar mixture crystallised in the pan
items.solution: The glucose syrup prevents crystallisation. If you are not using glucose, brush the inside of the pan above the syrup line with cold water once during cooking to dissolve any crystals that form.

items.problem: Turrón went soft after a few days
items.solution: Humidity absorbed into the turrón. Store airtight in a dry place. In damp weather, use a tin with a tight-fitting lid and a silica gel sachet if you have one.
